[FRONT_MATTER] [MINERU_DISCARDED_META] polymers MDPI Polymers 2022, 14, 4078. https://doi.org/10.3390/polym14194078 https://www.mdpi.com/journal/polymers [/MINERU_DISCARDED_META] Article # Methylethynyl-Terminated Polyimide Nanofibrous Membranes: High-Temperature-Resistant Adhesives with Low-Temperature Processability Haoran Qi †, Xi Ren †, Yuang Liu, Shengwei Dai, Changxu Yang, Xiaolei Wang and Jingang Liu * School of Materials Science and Technology, China University of Geosciences, Beijing 100083, China * Correspondence: liujg@cugb.edu.cn; Tel.: +86-10-8232-2972 † These authors contributed equally to this work. ![待补充图片](https://placeholder.pics/svg/1x1/FFFFFF/FFFFFF?text=暂无图片) check for updates Citation: Qi, H.; Ren, X.; Liu, Y.; Dai, S.; Yang, C.; Wang, X.; Liu, J. Methylethynyl-Terminated Polyimide Nanofibrous Membranes: High-Temperature-Resistant Adhesives with Low-Temperature Processability. Polymers 2022, 14, 4078. https://doi.org/10.3390/ polym14194078 Academic Editor: Irfad Kammakakam Received: 2 September 2022 Accepted: 24 September 2022 Published: 28 September 2022 Publisher's Note: MDPI stays neutral with regard to jurisdictional claims in published maps and institutional affiliations. ![待补充图片](https://placeholder.pics/svg/1x1/FFFFFF/FFFFFF?text=暂无图片) Copyright: © 2022 by the authors. Licensee MDPI, Basel, Switzerland. This article is an open access article distributed under the terms and conditions of the Creative Commons Attribution (CC BY) license (https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by/4.0/). Abstract: As an alternative to traditional riveting and welding materials, high-temperature-resistant adhesives, with their unique advantages, have been widely used in aviation, aerospace, and other fields. Among them, polyimide (PI) adhesives have been one of the most studied species both from basic and practical application aspects. However, in the main applications of solvent-type PI adhesives, pinholes or bubbles often exist in the cured PI adhesive layers due to the solvent volatilization and dehydration reaction, which directly affect the adhesive performance. To address this issue, electrospun PI nanofibrous membranes (NFMs) were employed as solvent-free or solventless adhesives for stainless steel in the current work. To enhance the adhesion of PI adhesives to the metal substrates, phenolphthalein groups and flexible ether bonds were introduced into the main chain of PIs via the monomers of $4,4^{\prime}$ -oxydiphthalic anhydride (ODPA) and 3,3-bis[4-(4-aminophenoxy)phenyl] phthalide (BAPPT). At the same time, the methylethynyl group was used as the end-capping component, and the crosslinking reaction of the alkynyl group at high temperature further increased the adhesive strength of the PI adhesives. Three kinds of methylethynyl-terminated PI (METI) NFMs with the set molecular weights of 5000, 10,000, and $20,000\mathrm{g / mol}$ were first prepared via the one-step high-temperature polycondensation procedure. Then, the PI NFMs were fabricated via the standard electrospinning procedure from the soluble METI solutions. The afforded METI NFMs showed excellent melt-flowing behaviors at high temperature. Incorporation of the methylethynyl end-capping achieved a crosslinking reaction at $280 - 310^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ for the NFMs, which was about $70^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ lower than those of the phenylacetylene end-capping counterparts. Using the METI NFMs as adhesive, stainless steel adherends were successfully bonded, and the single-lap shear strength (LSS) was higher than $20.0\mathrm{MPa}$ at both room temperature $(25^{\circ}\mathrm{C})$ and high temperature $(200^{\circ}\mathrm{C})$ . Keywords: polyimide; electrospinning; methylethynyl; adhesives; lap shear strength [EXPERIMENT] # 2. Materials and Methods # 2.1. Materials $4,4^{\prime}$ -Oxydiphthalic anhydride (ODPA, Changzhou Sunshine Pharmaceutical Co., Ltd., Changzhou, China) and 4-methylethynyl phthalic anhydride (MEPA, Nexam Chemical, Lomma, Sweden) were dried at $180~^\circ \mathrm{C}$ and $120~^\circ \mathrm{C}$ for $10\mathrm{h}$ , respectively, prior to use. 3,3-bis[4-(4-aminophenoxy)phenyl]phthalide (BAPPT) was synthesized in our laboratory according to the literature [29]. Electronic-grade $N,N$ -dimethylacetamide (DMAc), $N,N$ -dimethylformamide (DMF), and $N$ -methylpyrrolidone (NMP), purity $\geq 99.8\%$ , $\mathrm{Na^{+}} \leq 2$ ppm, water content $\leq 200$ ppm (Grinda Electronic Materials Co., Ltd., Hangzhou, China) were used directly. # 2.2. Characterization and Methods The inherent viscosity of METI resins was measured at $25^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ using an Ubbelohde viscometer (As-one, Osaka, Japan), and the absolute viscosity was measured at $25^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ using a DV2-TRVCP viscometer (Brookfield, Middleboro, MA, USA). Molecular weights of METI resins, including number-average molecular weight $(\mathbf{M}_{\mathrm{n}})$ and weight-average molecular weight $(\mathbf{M}_{\mathrm{w}})$ , were determined using an LC-20AD-type gel permeation chromatography (GPC, Shimadzu Corporation, Kyoto, Japan) system. Solubility tests were characterized as follows: $1.0\mathrm{g}$ of METI resins was mixed with $9.0\mathrm{g}$ of solvent (solid content $10\mathrm{wt}\%$ ) and stirred at room temperature for $24\mathrm{h}$ . Its solubility was visually judged and divided into three grades: completely dissolved $(++)$ , partially dissolved $(+ -)$ , and insoluble $(-)$ , where complete dissolution means that the resin is uniformly dispersed in the solution, transparent, without phase separation, precipitation, or gel formation; insolubilization indicates no change in the appearance of the resin. Attenuated total reflection-Fourier transform infrared (ATR-FTIR) spectra of PI NFMs were measured by a Bruker Tensor-27 FT-IR spectrometer (Bruker, Billerica, MA, USA), wavenumber range: $4000 - 400\mathrm{cm}^{-1}$ . Wide-angle X-ray diffraction (XRD) spectra were measured with a D/max-2500 X-ray diffractometer (Rigaku Corporation, Tokyo, Japan), Cu-Kα1 radiation, operating at $40\mathrm{kV}$ and $200\mathrm{mA}$ . Field-emission scanning electron microscope (FE-SEM) imaging was performed using a JSM-6700F (JEOL, Tokyo, Japan) with an accelerating voltage of $15\mathrm{kV}$ , and Pt/Pd was sputtered on each film before measurement. Ultraviolet-visible spectroscopy (UV-Vis) was performed at room temperature using a U-3210 spectrophotometer (Hitachi, Tokyo, Japan) for testing, and the PI fibrous membrane was dried at $100^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ for $10\mathrm{h}$ before the test to remove the adsorbed moisture. Thermogravimetric analysis (TGA) of PI NFMs was tested by a TA-Q50 thermal analysis system (Perkin-Elmer, Waltham, MA, USA) at a test temperature range of $30 - 760^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ and a heating rate of $20^{\circ}\mathrm{C / min}$ ; the test environment was nitrogen and the gas flow rate was $20\mathrm{mL / min}$ . Differential scanning calorimetry (DSC) was tested with a DSC 214 calorimetric differential scanner (NETZSCH, Selb, Germany); the test temperature range was $30 - 400^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ , the heating rate was $10^{\circ}\mathrm{C / min}$ , and the test environment was nitrogen with a gas flow of $20\mathrm{mL / min}$ . The rheological evaluation was carried out on an AR2000 rheometer (TA Instrument, Newcastle, DE, USA). METI powder was pressed to obtain a sample disk (diameter: $25\mathrm{mm}$ , thickness: $1.5 \pm 0.2\mathrm{mm}$ ). During the measurement, METI discs were loaded into parallel plates. The top parallel plate oscillated at a fixed angular frequency of $0.5\mathrm{Hz}$ and a fixed strain of $1.0\%$ ; the data acquisition range was $100 - 440^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ and the heating rate was $4^{\circ}\mathrm{C / min}$ . The single-lap shear strength (LSS) test was performed on an Instron 5567 tensile machine (Instron, Boston, MA, USA). The elongation speed was $2\mathrm{mm / min}$ . # 2.3. Synthesis of METI Resins Synthesis of soluble PI resin: METI resin was prepared by a high-temperature polycondensation method, using ODPA, BAPPT, and MEPA as raw materials, by controlling the ratio of the three raw materials. METI resins with molecular weights of $5000\mathrm{g / mol}$ , $10,000\mathrm{g / mol}$ , and $20,000\mathrm{g / mol}$ were designed and named METI-5K, 10K, and 20K, respectively. The following presentation takes METI-20K as an example to introduce the synthesis process of METI resin in detail: BAPPT (19.3218 g, 38.6019 mmol) and NMP (100 g) were charged into a $500~\mathrm{mL}$ three-necked flask equipped with a mechanical stirrer, an electric heating bath, and a Dean-Stark trap and purged with nitrogen at room temperature, then ODPA (11.5094 g, 37.1019 mmol) was added to the solution and stirred for $5\mathrm{h}$ . MEPA (0.5585 g, 3 mmol) and NMP (25 g) were added at the same time, and the solid content of the reaction mixture was controlled to be about $20\mathrm{wt\%}$ . After stirring for another $14\mathrm{h}$ at room temperature, toluene (100 g) and isoquinoline (1.0 g) were then added to the solution after stirring for a further $14\mathrm{h}$ at room temperature. The reaction mixture was heated to $140 - 145^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ , and the by-product water generated by the reaction was removed by toluene/water azeotrope. The refluxing dehydration reaction was maintained for $16\mathrm{h}$ . Then, toluene was distilled from the reaction system until the internal temperature of the reaction reached $180^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ , and the reaction was carried out at $180^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ for $1\mathrm{h}$ . The solution was cooled to $70^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ and the resulting viscous solution was precipitated in excess aqueous ethanol $(10\mathrm{wt\%})$ . Finally, the precipitated METI-20K resin was dried at room temperature for $24\mathrm{h}$ , and then dried under vacuum at $130^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ for $24\mathrm{h}$ . Finally, white filamentous METI-20K resin was obtained, yield: $29.62\mathrm{g}(97.7\%)$ . The METI-10K and METI-5K resins were prepared according to a procedure similar to the one mentioned above, with the formula shown in Table 1. METI-5K: yield: $28.76\mathrm{g}$ $(95.8\%)$ . METI-10K: yield: $29.08\mathrm{g}$ $(96.9\%)$ . Table 1. Formula for the METI-5K and METI-10K synthesis.

In addition, PI-ref (ODPA-BAPPT) resin without end-capping was synthesized according to the above method, and the reactant amounts of BAPPT and ODPA were $26.0781\mathrm{g}$ (52.1 mmol) and $16.1725\mathrm{g}$ (52.1 mmol), respectively. Yield: $39.41\mathrm{g}$ $(97.3\%)$ . # 2.4. Preparation of METI NFMs Taking METI-20K NFM as an example, the detailed preparation process of METI NFMs is as follows: The completely dried METI-20K resin was dissolved in DMAc solution, and the solid content of the solution was controlled at $33\mathrm{wt}\%$ to obtain a solution with an absolute viscosity of $5000\mathrm{mPa}$ s. The obtained METI-20K solution was uniformly injected into a $5\mathrm{mL}$ syringe using a spinneret with an inner diameter of $0.21\mathrm{mm}$ . The METI-20K solution was extruded from the spinneret with a syringe pump at a rate of $0.2\mathrm{mL/h}$ . The positive voltage between the injector and the collector was $16\mathrm{kV}$ and the negative voltage was $-3\mathrm{kV}$ . The distance between the spinneret and the grounded drum collector (diameter: $10\mathrm{cm}$ ; length: $30\mathrm{cm}$ ) was $15\mathrm{cm}$ . The relative humidity of the electrospinning device was controlled at $30\pm 5\%$ , and the speed of the collector was set at $2\mathrm{rpm}$ . METI-20K fibers were densely wound and deposited on aluminum foil. The resulting METI-20K NFM was vacuum-dried at $120^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ for $1\mathrm{h}$ to remove residual solvent. METI-5K and METI-10K NFMs were prepared following the same procedure as above, but starting with either a METI-5K or METI-10K solution with a solid content of $47.5\mathrm{wt}\%$ or $38\mathrm{wt}\%$ (absolute viscosity: $\sim 5000\mathrm{mPa}$ s), respectively, in the electrospinning solution. # 2.5. Preparation of Stainless Steel Samples Adhered with the METI NFMs The stainless steel adherends $(100\mathrm{mm}\times 25.4\mathrm{mm}\times 2\mathrm{mm},$ length $\times$ width $\times$ height) used for the lap shear stress (LSS) test were pretreated by polishing with standard sandpaper before use. The METI UFMs were cut into small pieces $(12.5\mathrm{mm}\times 25.4\mathrm{mm}$ length $\times$ width), stacked, and placed between two stainless steel adherends. The thickness of the final cured METI NFMs was controlled to be $0.3 - 0.5\mathrm{mm}$ . The overlapping area was $12.5\mathrm{mm}\times 25.4\mathrm{mm}$ (length $\times$ width). The METI NFMs were tightly clamped to the overlap of two stainless steel sheets. Subsequently, the METI NFM-adhered specimens were placed into an oven and then heated at $80^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ , $150^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ , $220^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ , $290^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ , $310^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ , and $330^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ each for $1\mathrm{h}$ . Then, the samples were cooled to room temperature. The LSS values at room temperature $(25^{\circ}\mathrm{C})$ were measured according to standard of GB/T 7124-2008 [30], "Determination of tensile shear strength of adhesives (rigid material vs. rigid material)". The high-temperature $(200^{\circ}\mathrm{C})$ LSS tests were carried out according to the standard of GJB 444-1988 [31], "Test method for high temperature tensile shear strength of adhesives (metal to metal)". In each LSS test, five specimens were tested and the average data were recorded. [RESULTS] # 3. Results and Discussion # 3.1. Molecular Weight and Solubility The preparation process of three METI resins is shown in Figure 1. The METI resins were named "METI-5K", "METI-10K", and "METI-20K" for the systems with designed molecular weights of $5000\mathrm{g / mol}$ , $10,000\mathrm{g / mol}$ , and $20,000\mathrm{g / mol}$ , respectively. First, the methylethynyl-terminated polyamic acid (MEPA-PAA) was synthesized using ODPA, BAPPT, and MEPA as the starting materials. Then, the soluble METI resins were afforded after dehydration at $180^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ . At last, METI-20K was obtained as continuous fibrous resin, METI-10K was obtained as short rod-like resin, and METI-5K was obtained as granular resin, indicating the gradually decreasing molecular weights of the resins. ![待补充图片](https://placeholder.pics/svg/1x1/FFFFFF/FFFFFF?text=暂无图片) Figure 1. Preparation of METI resins. The $\mathrm{M}_{\mathrm{n}}$ values of the METI resins shown in Table 2 are $1.59\times 10^{4}\mathrm{g / mol}$ for METI-5K, $1.71\times 10^{4}\mathrm{g / mol}$ for METI-10K, and $3.53\times 10^{4}\mathrm{g / mol}$ for METI-20K. It can be deduced from the data that all the measured $\mathrm{M}_{\mathrm{n}}$ values were higher than the designed values. This is consistent with the literature [32,33], which is mainly because the lithium bromide (LiBr) and phosphoric acid $(\mathrm{H}_3\mathrm{PO}_4)$ in the GPC mobile phase affect the solubility of METI resins in NMP, resulting in an increase in the measured values. The METI resins exhibited narrow PDI values of 1.36 for METI-5K, 2.12 for METI-10K, and 1.66 for METI-20K. In addition, the $[\eta ]_{\mathrm{inh}}$ and the $\mathrm{M}_{\mathrm{n}}$ values of the controlled PI-ref (ODPA-BAPPT) resin were $1.21~\mathrm{dL / g}$ and $19.0\times 10^{4}\mathrm{g / mol}$ , respectively, indicating the high reactivity of the monomers. The moderate-to-high molecular weight feature of the METI resins, on one hand, could afford good melting flowability at elevated temperatures and, on the other hand, could guarantee good mechanical properties of the afforded electrospun NFMs. Table 2. All three METI resins exhibited good solubility in polar aprotic solvents, as shown in Table 1. They were all soluble in NMP, DMAc, and DMF at room temperature with a solid content of $10\%$ . They were also partially soluble in chloroform $(\mathrm{CHCl}_3)$ and tetrahydrofuran (THF). The PI-ref resin was insoluble in tetrahydrofuran. The good solubility of the METI resins in the test solvents, on one hand, came from the controlled molecular weights and, on the other hand, from the flexible ether linkages and bulky phenolphthalein groups in the molecular chains of the resins. The viscosity-solid content relationship curves of the three METI resins are shown in Figure 2. There are clear differences in the solubility of the METI resins with different molecular weights in the same DMAc solvent. It can be seen that the viscosity of the METI resins gradually decreased with the decreasing molecular weights at the same solid content. In order to afford PI NFMs with fine micromorphologies, the METI solutions with the absolute viscosities of $5000\mathrm{mPa}$ were prepared for the following electrospinning fabrications. According to the figure, different solid contents that were $47.5\mathrm{wt}\%$ for METI-5K, $38.0\mathrm{wt}\%$ for METI-10K, and $33.0\mathrm{wt}\%$ for METI-20K were chosen. ![待补充图片](https://placeholder.pics/svg/1x1/FFFFFF/FFFFFF?text=暂无图片) Figure 2. Viscosity-solid content relationship of METI solutions with DMAc as solvent at room temperature. # 3.2. Electrospinning Fabrications of METI NFMs and the Micromorphology A series of METI NFMs were fabricated according to the electrospinning procedures shown in Figure 3 with the parameters shown in Section 2.4. It can be seen from the SEM images of the METI NFMs, shown in Figure 4, that the NFMs with fine micromorphology were successfully prepared. The flexible and tough fibrous membranes were composed of tangled nanofibers with average diameters $(\mathrm{d_{av}})$ from $644~\mathrm{nm}$ (METI-20K) to $1471~\mathrm{nm}$ (METI-5K). At the same viscosity, with a decrease in the designed molecular weights, the $\mathrm{d_{av}}$ values gradually increased. This might be attributed to the higher solid content of the resin solution with lower molecular weights at the same viscosity, resulting in higher resin content in the spinning solution sprayed from the spinneret per unit of time. Macroscopically, the $\mathrm{d_{av}}$ values became larger. ![待补充图片](https://placeholder.pics/svg/1x1/FFFFFF/FFFFFF?text=暂无图片) (a) ![待补充图片](https://placeholder.pics/svg/1x1/FFFFFF/FFFFFF?text=暂无图片) (b) (c) ![待补充图片](https://placeholder.pics/svg/1x1/FFFFFF/FFFFFF?text=暂无图片) Figure 3. (a) Electrospinning process for METI NFMs; (b) Fabrication of stainless steel adherends and the lap shear strength (LSS) measurements; (c) Schematic diagram of the dimensions of stainless steel adherends for adhesion. ![待补充图片](https://placeholder.pics/svg/1x1/FFFFFF/FFFFFF?text=暂无图片) Figure 4. SEM images and average diameters $(\mathrm{d_{av}})$ of METI NFMs. (a) METI-5K; (b) METI-10K; (c) METI-20K. ![待补充图片](https://placeholder.pics/svg/1x1/FFFFFF/FFFFFF?text=暂无图片) ![待补充图片](https://placeholder.pics/svg/1x1/FFFFFF/FFFFFF?text=暂无图片) To confirm the chemical structures of the METI NFMs, FTIR measurements were performed on the fibrous membranes with a reflective mode. Figure 5a reveals the FTIR spectra of the pristine NFMs without heat treatment. The carbonyl asymmetric stretching vibration peak at $1778~\mathrm{cm}^{-1}$ , the asymmetric stretching vibration peak at $1716~\mathrm{cm}^{-1}$ , and the C-N bond stretching vibration peak at $1371~\mathrm{cm}^{-1}$ clearly prove the existence of the imide rings. Secondly, the phenolphthalein $-\mathrm{C(O)} - \mathrm{O}-$ asymmetric stretching vibration peak at $1608~\mathrm{cm}^{-1}$ and the characteristic absorption peaks of the ether bonds $(-O-)$ at $1234~\mathrm{cm}^{-1}$ were also detected. Since the contents of alkynyl groups $(\mathrm{C} \equiv \mathrm{C})$ in METIs were quite low and the absorption peaks were weaker, the absorption of the $\mathrm{C} \equiv \mathrm{C}$ groups in the wavenumber range of $2000 - 3000~\mathrm{cm}^{-1}$ was enlarged, shown in Figure 5b. Obvious stretching vibration peaks of the substituted $-\mathrm{C} \equiv \mathrm{C}-$ at $2238~\mathrm{cm}^{-1}$ were detected for all METI NFMs. Visually, the intensity of the $-\mathrm{C} \equiv \mathrm{C}-$ absorption peaks gradually decreases with increasing molecular weights for the polymers, which is mainly related to the decrease in alkynyl contents in the polymers. The above infrared absorption peaks demonstrate the successful preparation of METI NFMs. ![待补充图片](https://placeholder.pics/svg/1x1/FFFFFF/FFFFFF?text=暂无图片) Figure 5. FTIR spectra of METI NFMs. (a) spectra of pristine METI NFMs; (b) spectra in the wavenumber range of $2000 - 3000\mathrm{cm}^{-1}$ ; (c) spectra of METI-10K treated at different temperatures. ![待补充图片](https://placeholder.pics/svg/1x1/FFFFFF/FFFFFF?text=暂无图片) ![待补充图片](https://placeholder.pics/svg/1x1/FFFFFF/FFFFFF?text=暂无图片) Furthermore, the METI-10K NFM was heat-treated at different temperatures, and the FTIR spectra are shown in Figure 5c. With increasing temperatures, the characteristic absorption peaks of $-\mathrm{C}\equiv \mathrm{C}-$ groups gradually weakened and nearly disappeared at about $300^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ , which was mainly due to the crosslinking of the alkynyl groups at this temperature [34]. Thus, it can be roughly determined that the crosslinking reaction for the current METI-10 NFM occurred at around $300^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ . Obtained by the same method, the crosslinking temperatures of METI-20K and METI-5K samples were about $320^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ and $280^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ , respectively. # 3.3. Thermal Properties Two methods, TGA and DSC, were used to investigate the thermal stability of the METI NFMs at elevated temperatures and the data are summarized in Table 3. Figure 6 records the TGA and derivative TGA (DTG) curves of the METI NFMs. All three METI NFMs exhibit good thermal stability up to $450^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ . With a further increase in temperature, the NFMs began to decompose, reaching a $5\%$ weight-loss temperature $(T_{5\%})$ of $522.5^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ for METI-5K, $520.2^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ for METI-10K, and $534.8^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ for METI-20K. From the DTG curve, it can be clearly seen that METI-10K and METI-20K samples exhibited clear two-stage thermal decomposition behaviors. The first stage occurred at about $540^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ , which was mainly due to the decomposition of the side-chain phthalide groups. The second stage was recorded at about $600^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ , which was mainly attributed to decomposition of the main chains of the polymers. Two-stage decomposition was not observed for METI-5K, which might be due to the reduced thermal resistance caused by lower molecular weights [35]. The $700^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ residual weight ratio $(\mathrm{R}_{\mathrm{w}700})$ of the METI NFMs was about $64.6 - 65.9\mathrm{wt\%}$ . As expected, METI-20K NFM, with the highest molecular weights, showed the highest $\mathrm{R}_{\mathrm{w}700}$ value. Table 3. Thermal properties of METI NFMs.

TGA and DTG plots of METI NFMs in nitrogen atmosphere. The curing process of the METI NFMs was further investigated by DSC measurements and the results are shown in Figure 7. As shown in Figure 7a, the METI polymers showed a clear two-stage transition process in the first heating scan. In the first stage, the polymers began melting to form endothermic peaks at $230.2^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ , $244.4^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ , and $253.5^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ for METI-5K, METI-10K, and METI-20K samples, respectively. Then, with the increase in temperature, the second stages occurred at $276.1^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ , $301.9^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ , and $323.6^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ for the three METI NFMs, respectively. Apparently, the second transitions were due to the alkynyl crosslinking reactions. It is worth noticing that current METI polymers exhibited crosslinking temperatures $(T_{\mathrm{c}})$ in the range of $280 - 320^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ , which is nearly $70^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ lower than that of the conventional phenylethynyl $(C_6H_4 - C\equiv C - C_6H_4)$ crosslinker [36]. This would undoubtedly expand the applications of "low-temperature" curable alkynyl-terminated PIs. In addition, the $T_{\mathrm{c}}$ values of the METI NFMs increased gradually with an increase in the molecular weights of the polymers, that is, with a decrease in the alkynyl contents of the METI NFMs. Meanwhile, the endothermic peak intensities of the METI polymers also gradually weakened from METI-5K to METI-20K. The second DSC run was recorded and is shown in Figure 7b, in which the exothermic peaks of the thermally crosslinking reactions of METI NFMs all disappeared, indicating complete crosslinking in the polymers. Meanwhile, clear glass transitions were detected. The glass transition temperatures $(T_{\mathrm{g}})$ of the three thermally crosslinked METI NFMs were $270.5^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ for METI-5K, $272.0^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ for METI-10K, and $273.5^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ for METI-20K. On one hand, the existence of clear glass transitions in the crosslinked polymers meant that the degrees of the crosslinking reactions for the current METI polymers were relatively low, resulting in some extent of thermoplastic features for the cured METI polymers. On the other hand, the $T_{\mathrm{g}}$ values of the three METI polymers were a bit lower than that of the PI-ref $(T_{\mathrm{g}} = 288.7^{\circ}\mathrm{C})$ ; however, the molecular weights of the METI polymers had little effect on the molecular segments' motion at elevated temperatures. ![待补充图片](https://placeholder.pics/svg/1x1/FFFFFF/FFFFFF?text=暂无图片) Figure 7. DSC diagrams of METI NFMs in nitrogen atmosphere. (a) first DSC scan; (b) second DSC scan. ![待补充图片](https://placeholder.pics/svg/1x1/FFFFFF/FFFFFF?text=暂无图片) # 3.4. Adhesive Properties Generally, the ideal bonding process for the adherends requires the adhesive to have a certain melting flowability to achieve good wettability and infiltrability between the adherends. The determination of complex viscosities and $\mathrm{T_c}$ of the adhesives play important roles for achieving good adhesion [37]. Thus, rheological behaviors of the METI NFMs were evaluated. As shown in Figure 8a, all METIs exhibited good melting flowability, which was mainly attributed to the controlled molecular weights and also the decreased intermolecular interactions due to the incorporation of flexible ether bonds and bulky phthalide groups into the polymer molecular chains. When METI-5K, METI-10K, and METI-20K samples reached the minimum complex viscosities $(\eta_{\mathrm{min}})$ , the corresponding temperatures $(\mathrm{T}_{\mathrm{min}})$ were $268.8^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ (1413.81 Pa s), $318.5^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ (11,035.2 Pa s), and $344.1^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ (16,187.9 Pa s), respectively. At the same time, the $\eta_{\mathrm{min}}$ values gradually decreased with ta decrease in the molecular weights of the polymers, indicating that the decreased molecular weights were beneficial for the improvement of the melting flowability of the polymers. ![待补充图片](https://placeholder.pics/svg/1x1/FFFFFF/FFFFFF?text=暂无图片) Figure 8. Rheological curves of three molecular weight resins: (a) METI; (b) METI and PETI. ![待补充图片](https://placeholder.pics/svg/1x1/FFFFFF/FFFFFF?text=暂无图片) The rheological behaviors of the METI NFMs were further compared with the phenylethynyl-terminated (PETI) analogous materials [26] and the results are shown in Figure 8b. On one hand, the $\mathrm{T}_{\mathrm{min}}$ values of the METIs were apparently lower than those of the PETI counterparts with the same designed molecular weights. For example, the METI-5K exhibited a $\mathrm{T}_{\mathrm{min}}$ value of $268.8^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ , which was $89.6^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ lower than that of the PETI-5K sample $(\mathrm{T}_{\mathrm{min}} = 358.4^{\circ}\mathrm{C})$ . From another point, the temperature required for achieving the same melting viscosity for the METI sample was also significantly lower than that of the PETI sample. The low-temperature crosslinkable feature for the METI NFMs was helpful for the following adhesion applications. To further explore the effects of processing temperature on the melting flowability of the METI NFMs, hot-pressing experiments were performed. The detailed evaluation procedure was reported in our previous work [26]. The double-layer fibrous membranes were placed into a heat-sealing machine, and the pressure was set to $0.5\mathrm{MPa}$ . The hot-pressing procedure was performed at different temperatures for $60~\mathrm{s}$ . The loading amounts of the METI NFMs on per bonded surface unit were about $30\sim 40\mathrm{mg}$ . The SEM images of the METI NFMs after hot pressing are shown in Figures 9-11. In all three of the figures, the upper left corner of the group (b) shows the appearance of METI NFMs after hot pressing, the left side of the separating red line represents the un-heated part, and the right side is after hot pressing. It can be observed that the fibrous structures of the right part for the NFMs all gradually transformed into uniform film-like structures with increasing hot-pressing temperatures. Meanwhile, the right bi-layer fibrous membranes gradually became transparent, indicating the fusion of the two layers. This interface morphology, which is fibrous on the left and fused film on the right, can be clearly observed in the SEM images shown in Figures 9a, 10a and 11a with a relatively lower magnitude of enlargement. ![待补充图片](https://placeholder.pics/svg/1x1/FFFFFF/FFFFFF?text=暂无图片) Figure 9. SEM images of METI-5K NFM after hot-pressing treatment. (a) $500 \times$ ; (b) $4000 \times$ . ![待补充图片](https://placeholder.pics/svg/1x1/FFFFFF/FFFFFF?text=暂无图片) Figure 10. SEM images of METI-10K NFM after hot-pressing treatment. (a) $500\times$ ; (b) $4000\times$ . For the METI-5K sample (Figure 9), when the hot-pressing temperature reached about $290^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ , the two NFM layers were almost completely fused into one uniform film structure; while for the METI-10K and METI-20K samples, the temperatures were $310^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ and $330^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ , respectively. The results were consistent with the rheological test results shown in Figure 8. Compared with our previous work [26], the melting temperatures of METI NFMs were greatly decreased, which was mainly attributed to the lower crosslinking temperature of flexible propynyl groups compared with the rigid phenylethynyl end-cappers. The processing temperatures for the final bonding procedures were then set as follows: METI-5K: $290^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ ; METI-10K: $310^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ ; and METI-20K: $330^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ . ![待补充图片](https://placeholder.pics/svg/1x1/FFFFFF/FFFFFF?text=暂无图片) Figure 11. SEM images of METI-20K NFM after hot-pressing treatment. (a) $500 \times$ ; (b) $4000 \times$ . Finally, the single-lap shear strength (LSS) tests were used to evaluate the adhesion performance of the current METI NFMs to the stainless steel adherends [38]. The results are shown in Figure 12, and the data are summarized in Table 4. The three kinds of fibrous membrane adhesives showed good adhesion performance to the stainless steel adherends both at room temperature and at a high temperature of $200^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ . The LSS values were higher than $25\mathrm{MPa}$ at room temperature and higher than $20\mathrm{MPa}$ at $200^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ , and were all apparently higher than those of the PI-ref adhesive. This was due to the synergistic effects of the flexible ether linkages, phenolphthalein side chains, and the propynyl terminators. Among them, METI-10K showed the highest bonding strength $(\mathrm{LSS}_{25} = 29.6\mathrm{MPa};\mathrm{LSS}_{200} = 26.2\mathrm{MPa})$ due to its excellent comprehensive properties, including suitable contents of alkynyl endcappers and moderate molecular weight. Furthermore, the bonding temperature for the METI-10K samples was only $310^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ . Compared with our previous work [26], the processing temperature of the current METI-10K decreases nearly $70^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ lower when compared to the analogous PETI-10K sample $(380^{\circ}\mathrm{C})$ . The bonding strength was comparable to those of the PETI ones. This would reduce the difficulty in practical bonding fabrications and improve the bonding efficiency. ![待补充图片](https://placeholder.pics/svg/1x1/FFFFFF/FFFFFF?text=暂无图片) Figure 12. Lap shear strength (LSS) of electrospun METI fiber membranes for bonding stainless steel sheets. Table 4. Bonding strength of METI NFM adhesives.

source_refs.section_title: Alicyclic Polyimide/SiO $_2$  Mixed Matrix Membranes for Water/n-Butanol Pervaporation

chain_input.text:
[FRONT_MATTER] [MINERU_DISCARDED_META] A membranes MDPI Membranes 2021, 11, 564. https://doi.org/10.3390/membranes11080564 https://www.mdpi.com/journal/membranes [/MINERU_DISCARDED_META] Article # Alicyclic Polyimide/SiO $_2$ Mixed Matrix Membranes for Water/n-Butanol Pervaporation Ching-Wen Hsieh $^{1}$ , Bo-Xian Li $^{1}$ and Shing-Yi Suen $^{1,2,*}$ $^{1}$ Department of Chemical Engineering, National Chung Hsing University, Taichung 402, Taiwan; d100065008@mail.nchu.edu.tw (C.-W.H.); sa4104065009@smail.nchu.edu.tw (B.-X.L.) 2 i-Center for Advanced Science and Technology, National Chung Hsing University, Taichung 402, Taiwan * Correspondence: sysuen@nchu.edu.tw Abstract: Alicyclic polyimides (PIs) have excellent properties in solubility, mechanical strength, thermal property, etc. This study developed two types of alicyclic PI-based mixed matrix membranes (MMMs) for water/n-butanol pervaporation application, which have never been investigated previously. The fillers were hydrophilic $\mathrm{SiO}_2$ nanoparticles. The synthesized PI was mixed with $\mathrm{SiO}_2$ nanoparticles in DMAc to make the casting solution, and a liquid film was formed over PET substrate using doctor blade. A dense MMM was fabricated at $80^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ and further treated via multistage curing $(100 - 170^{\circ}\mathrm{C})$ . The prepared membranes were characterized by FTIR, TGA, FE-SEM, water contact angle, and solvent swelling. The trends of pure solvent swelling effects agree well with the water contact angle results. Moreover, the pervaporation efficiencies of alicyclic PI/ $\mathrm{SiO}_2$ MMMs for $85\mathrm{wt}\%$ n-butanol aqueous solution at $40^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ were investigated. The results showed that BCDA-3,4'-ODA/ $\mathrm{SiO}_2$ MMMs had a larger permeation flux and higher separation factor than BCDA-1,3,3-APB/ $\mathrm{SiO}_2$ MMMs. For both types of MMMs, the separation factor increased first and then decreased, with increasing $\mathrm{SiO}_2$ loading. Based on the PSI performance, the optimal $\mathrm{SiO}_2$ content was $0.5\mathrm{wt}\%$ for BCDA-3,4'-ODA/ $\mathrm{SiO}_2$ MMMs and $5\mathrm{wt}\%$ for BCDA-1,3,3-APB/ $\mathrm{SiO}_2$ MMMs. The overall separation efficiency of BCDA-3,4'-ODA-based membranes was 10-30-fold higher. Keywords: mixed matrix membrane; alicyclic polyimide; pervaporation ![待补充图片](https://placeholder.pics/svg/1x1/FFFFFF/FFFFFF?text=暂无图片) check for updates Citation: Hsieh, C.-W.; Li, B.-X.; Suen, S.-Y. Alicyclic Polyimide/SiO $_2$ Mixed Matrix Membranes for Water/n-Butanol Pervaporation. Membranes 2021, 11, 564. https://doi.org/10.3390/ membranes11080564 Academic Editor: Alexander Toikka Received: 22 June 2021 Accepted: 23 July 2021 Published: 27 July 2021 Publisher's Note: MDPI stays neutral with regard to jurisdictional claims in published maps and institutional affiliations. ![待补充图片](https://placeholder.pics/svg/1x1/FFFFFF/FFFFFF?text=暂无图片) Copyright: © 2021 by the authors. Licensee MDPI, Basel, Switzerland. This article is an open access article distributed under the terms and conditions of the Creative Commons Attribution (CC BY) license (https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by/4.0/). [EXPERIMENT] # 2. Materials and Methods # 2.1. Materials Bicyclo[2.2.2]oct-7-ene-2,3,5,6-tetracarboxylic dianhydride (BCDA), $3,4^{\prime}$ -oxydianiline $(3,4^{\prime}$ ODA), and 1,3-bis(3-aminophenoxy)benzene (1,3,3-APB) were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich (St. Louis, MO, USA) and used without further purification. $\gamma$ -Butyrolactone (GBL) and N,N-dimethylacetamide (DMAc) from Sigma-Aldrich were selected as solvents and dried overnight with molecular sieves prior to use. Colloidal silica solution, which contains ca. $20~\mathrm{wt\%}$ of $20~\mathrm{nm}~\mathrm{SiO}_2$ nanoparticles in DMAc, was provided from Nissan Chemical (Tokyo Japan). # 2.2. Preparation of PIs The PI synthesis process is depicted in Figure 1. The molar ratio of BCDA-3,4'-ODA or BCDA-1,3,3-APB was 1. The monomers and 4-fold weight of co-solvent (DMAc/GBL = 60/40 $(w / w)$ ) were added to a three-necked flask equipped with a mechanical stirrer, a thermometer, and two connected condensers. The flask was purged with nitrogen, and the mixture was stirred at room temperature for $2\mathrm{h}$ to react and create a homogeneous poly(amic acid) (PAA) solution. The catalyst isoquinoline $(2\mathrm{wt}\%)$ was then added into the three-necked flask, and the temperature was increased to $170^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ . The solution was refluxed at $170^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ for $16\mathrm{h}$ until the imidization of PAA was completed. The product solution was slowly cooled down, and excess ethanol was poured into the solution for PI precipitation in order to separate it from the unreacted monomers or low-molecular-weight PI. Finally, the PI precipitate was dried in a vacuum oven at $150^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ for $8\mathrm{h}$ . ![待补充图片](https://placeholder.pics/svg/1x1/FFFFFF/FFFFFF?text=暂无图片) Figure 1. Synthetic routes of alicyclic PIs, BCDA-3,4'-ODA, and BCDA-1,3,3-APB. # 2.3. Preparation and Characterization of $\mathsf{PI} / \mathsf{SiO}_2$ Mixed Matrix Membranes 20 wt% PI was dissolved in DMAc at room temperature for 2 h. The PI solution was mixed with the colloidal silica solution at a certain ratio for 5 min to make the casting solution. The casting solution was then spread over a clean PET substrate using a $250~\mu \mathrm{m}$ blade to form a liquid film. The liquid film with the PET substrate was placed in an oven at $80^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ for $1\mathrm{h}$ to fabricate a dense membrane. The membrane was peeled off from PET and further treated via multi-stage curing. The curing temperature was $100^{\circ}\mathrm{C}, 140^{\circ}\mathrm{C}, 150^{\circ}\mathrm{C},$ and $160^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ , and the curing time was $1\mathrm{h}$ for each stage. In the final stage, the temperature was raised to $170^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ and the membrane was cured for $5\mathrm{h}$ . The preparation procedures for pristine PI membranes were similar to the above for $\mathrm{PI} / \mathrm{SiO}_2$ MMM, without the blending of $\mathrm{SiO}_2$ nanoparticles. The prepared membranes were characterized using a thickness gauge (293-140-30, Mitutoyo, Kanagawa, Japan), FTIR (Cary 630, Agilent, Tokyo, Japan), TGA (Q500, TA Instruments, New Castle, DE, USA), FE-SEM (JSM-6700F, Jeol, Tokyo, Japan), and contact angle (SEO: Phoenix-I portable contact angle analyzer). # 2.4. Swelling Experiment The prepared membrane was cut into a size of $3\mathrm{cm} \times 3\mathrm{cm}$ and dried in an oven for $24\mathrm{h}$ . The membrane weight was recorded as dry weight $W_{\mathrm{d}}$ (g). Next, the membrane was soaked into pure alcohol (n-butanol or ethanol) or pure deionized water at $40^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ . After $6\mathrm{h}$ , the membrane was taken out and the remaining liquid on the membrane surface was wiped. The membrane weight was recorded as wet weight $W_{\mathrm{s}}$ (g). The degree of swelling (DS) of the membrane was then calculated with the following equation [51]: $$ \mathrm{DS}(\%) = \frac {W _ {\mathrm{s}} - W _ {\mathrm{d}}}{W _ {\mathrm{d}}} \times 100 \% . \tag{1} $$ # 2.5. Pervaporation Experiment In the pervaporation process, $1\mathrm{L}$ of $85~\mathrm{wt\%}$ n-butanol aqueous solution at $40^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ was poured into the feed container and pumped into the module with a piece of membrane disc (effective surface area: $19.63~\mathrm{cm}^2$ ). The feed solution passed in the upstream at a flow rate of $9\mathrm{L / h}$ , and circulated back to the feed container. The permeate was vacuumed and collected in a cold trap. The experimental set-up is illustrated in Figure 2. The duration of pervaporation process was $6\mathrm{h}$ . The compositions of the solution remained in the feed container and the permeate were analyzed by HPLC. The HPLC system consisted of a pump (600 Controller, Waters), a refractive index detector (RI-101, Shodex), a C8 column (Luna $5\mu \mathrm{m}$ C8(2) $100\AA$ , Phenomenex), and a computer with integration software installed. The mobile phase was pure water, and flow rate was $0.5\mathrm{mL / min}$ . For each injection, the sample amount was $20~\mu \mathrm{L}$ . ![待补充图片](https://placeholder.pics/svg/1x1/FFFFFF/FFFFFF?text=暂无图片) Figure 2. Experimental set-up for pervaporation process. The total flux permeating through the membrane was calculated as follows: $$ J = \frac {W}{A t}, \tag {2} $$ where $W$ is the total mass of the permeate (g), $A$ is the effective membrane area $(\mathrm{m}^2)$ , and $t$ is the duration time of the experiment (h). Considering that the permeation flux is usually affected by membrane thickness, a normalized permeation flux $(J_N)$ may be expressed in terms of a certain membrane thickness $(L_N$ , the average membrane thickness of $30~\mu \mathrm{m}$ was adopted in this study) for normalization, as given in the following: $$ J _ {N} = \frac {J L}{L _ {N}}, \tag {3} $$ where L is the thickness of the membrane adopted in pervaporation process. The separation factor was determined as: $$ \alpha = \frac {\mathrm {y} / (1 - \mathrm {y})}{\mathrm {x} / (1 - \mathrm {x})}, \tag {4} $$ where $y$ and $x$ represent the weight fractions of water in the permeate and feed, respectively. In addition, the overall performance of the membrane, combining permeation flux and separation factor together, could be evaluated by pervaporation separation index (PSI) as: $$ \mathrm {P S I} = \mathrm {J} _ {\mathrm {N}} (\alpha - 1). \tag {5} $$ [RESULTS] # 3. Results and Discussion # 3.1. Membrane Characterization Figure 3 presents the FTIR spectra of pristine alicyclic PI membranes and their related $\mathrm{PI} / \mathrm{SiO}_2$ MMMs. In comparison with the pristine PI membranes, the characteristic peaks of Si-O-Si at $1080~\mathrm{cm^{-1}}$ and $\mathrm{Si - O(H)}$ at $940~\mathrm{cm^{-1}}$ [52-54] were observed for all the $\mathrm{PI} / \mathrm{SiO}_2$ MMMs. It verified the successful incorporation of $\mathrm{SiO}_2$ nanoparticles in alicyclic PI-based matrices. ![待补充图片](https://placeholder.pics/svg/1x1/FFFFFF/FFFFFF?text=暂无图片) Figure 3. FTIR spectra of pristine alicyclic PI membranes and their related $\mathrm{PI} / \mathrm{SiO}_2$ mixed matrix membranes. ![待补充图片](https://placeholder.pics/svg/1x1/FFFFFF/FFFFFF?text=暂无图片) According to the TGA results in Figure 4, both pristine alicyclic PI membranes (BCDA-3,4'-ODA and BCDA-1,3,3-APB) exhibited excellent thermal stability because their temperatures for $5\%$ weight loss $(T_{5\%})$ were higher than $350^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ . Compared to pristine alicyclic PI membranes, the TGA curves for $\mathrm{PI} / \mathrm{SiO}_2$ MMMs shifted toward slightly higher temperatures. It implies that the thermal stability of the membrane was enhanced with the incorporation of inorganic $\mathrm{SiO}_2$ nanoparticles. ![待补充图片](https://placeholder.pics/svg/1x1/FFFFFF/FFFFFF?text=暂无图片) Figure 4. TGA results of pristine alicylic PI membranes and their related $\mathrm{PI} / \mathrm{SiO}_2$ mixed matrix membranes. ![待补充图片](https://placeholder.pics/svg/1x1/FFFFFF/FFFFFF?text=暂无图片) The residual $\mathrm{wt\%}$ of $\mathrm{PI} / \mathrm{SiO}_2$ MMM in Figure 4 was contributed from the undecomposed $\mathrm{SiO}_2$ nanoparticles, and the value was elevated with the increasing $\mathrm{SiO}_2$ content. The actual $\mathrm{SiO}_2$ $\mathrm{wt\%}$ in $\mathrm{PI} / \mathrm{SiO}_2$ MMM was then calculated from the residual $\mathrm{wt\%}$ values of the relevant TGA curves as follows: $$ \mathrm{SiO}_{2}\mathrm{wt}\% = \frac{\mathrm{Y} - \mathrm{X}}{\mathrm{W} - \mathrm{X}}\times 100\%, \tag{6} $$ where $\mathrm{Y} =$ residual wt% of PI/SiO $_2$ MMM, $\mathrm{X} =$ residual wt% of pristine PI membrane, $\mathrm{W} =$ residual wt% of SiO $_2$ particles. The results are listed in Table 1. The actual SiO $_2$ contents in PI/SiO $_2$ MMMs were a little greater than the values used in the experiments of the membrane preparation. It is possible that some polymer was vanished with solvent vaporization during the curing stage so that the resulted inorganic filler content in MMM was increased. In the subsequent text of this paper, the values of SiO $_2$ wt% adopted in the experiment are presented, since the actual contents are close to them. Table 1. Values of actual ${\mathrm{{SiO}}}_{2}$ .wt% in alicyclic PI/SiO ${}_{2}$ MMMs obtained from TGA.
SiO2 wt% Used in Experiment0.5 wt%2.5 wt%5 wt%15 wt%
BCDA-3,4'-ODA/SiO2 MMM0.7 wt%2.6 wt%6.1 wt%17.5 wt%
BCDA-1,3,3-APB/SiO2 MMM0.6 wt%3.0 wt%7.1 wt%17.4 wt%
The thickness for pristine PI membranes and $\mathrm{PI} / \mathrm{SiO}_2$ MMMs ranged from 20 to $40~\mu \mathrm{m}$ such that the average thickness was $30~{\mu\mathrm{m}}$ . The membrane structures were observed via FE-SEM, and the cross-sectional images are displayed in Figure 5. When $0.5\mathrm{wt}\%$ $\mathrm{SiO}_2$ was filled into the PI matrix, clusters of several $\mathrm{SiO}_2$ nanoparticles were created. This phenomenon could be attributed to the particle-particle interaction formed via hydrogen bonding [43,55]. With increasing $\mathrm{SiO}_2$ content, particle agglomeration became more and more serious. As the $\mathrm{SiO}_2$ content was raised to $15\mathrm{wt}\%$ , large defects appeared due to the severe particle-cluster aggregation. A similar trend occurred in the literature with mesoporous $\mathrm{SiO}_2$ spheres $(1.8 - 2\mu \mathrm{m})$ dispersed into PVA membranes [56]. Because the membrane defects greatly deteriorated the pervaporation performance, the $\mathrm{PI} / 15\mathrm{wt}\%$ $\mathrm{SiO}_2$ MMMs were not suitable for pervaporation. ![待补充图片](https://placeholder.pics/svg/1x1/FFFFFF/FFFFFF?text=暂无图片) ![待补充图片](https://placeholder.pics/svg/1x1/FFFFFF/FFFFFF?text=暂无图片) ![待补充图片](https://placeholder.pics/svg/1x1/FFFFFF/FFFFFF?text=暂无图片) ![待补充图片](https://placeholder.pics/svg/1x1/FFFFFF/FFFFFF?text=暂无图片) ![待补充图片](https://placeholder.pics/svg/1x1/FFFFFF/FFFFFF?text=暂无图片) ![待补充图片](https://placeholder.pics/svg/1x1/FFFFFF/FFFFFF?text=暂无图片) ![待补充图片](https://placeholder.pics/svg/1x1/FFFFFF/FFFFFF?text=暂无图片) ![待补充图片](https://placeholder.pics/svg/1x1/FFFFFF/FFFFFF?text=暂无图片) ![待补充图片](https://placeholder.pics/svg/1x1/FFFFFF/FFFFFF?text=暂无图片) Figure 5. FE-SEM images of pristine alicyclic PI membranes and their related $\mathrm{PI} / \mathrm{SiO}_2$ mixed matrix membranes. ![待补充图片](https://placeholder.pics/svg/1x1/FFFFFF/FFFFFF?text=暂无图片) Table 2 presents the water contact angles of pristine alicyclic PI membranes and their related $\mathrm{PI} / \mathrm{SiO}_2$ MMMs. The PI with BCDA-3,4'-ODA had a lower contact angle (about $10^{\circ}$ lower) than that with BCDA-1,3,3-APB, indicating that BCDA-3,4'-ODA was more hydrophilic than BCDA-1,3,3-APB (with one more phenoxy group). However, the addition of $\mathrm{SiO}_2$ nanoparticles gave opposite effects on the water contact angle for both PIs. The water contact angle increased for the $\mathrm{SiO}_2$ incorporation on BCDA-3,4'-ODA/SiO $_2$ MMMs, while the value decreased in the case of BCDA-1,3,3-APB/SiO $_2$ MMMs. In most literatures incorporating $\mathrm{SiO}_2$ particles into dehydration pervaporation membranes [56-59], the water contact angle decreased with the increased $\mathrm{SiO}_2$ wt%. Our case of BCDA-1,3,3-APB/SiO $_2$ MMMs exhibited a similar tendency, which implies that BCDA-1,3,3-APB/SiO $_2$ MMMs became slightly more hydrophilic than the pristine BCDA-1,3,3-APB PI membrane [56-59]. On the contrary, the water contact angle for the composite coating via filling $\mathrm{SiO}_2$ nanoparticles into waterborne fluorine-containing epoxy was found to increase with the increased $\mathrm{SiO}_2$ content in the work of [60]; a superhydrophobic surface was created with the inclusion of fumed $\mathrm{SiO}_2$ nanoparticles inside a PDMS pervaporation membrane for ethanol recovery [61]. Similar to these results, the as-prepared BCDA-3,4'-ODA/SiO $_2$ MMMs was a little less hydrophilic than the pristine BCDA-3,4'-ODA PI membrane. Table 2. Water contact angles of pristine alicyclic PI membranes and their related $\mathrm{PI / SiO_2}$ mixed matrix membranes.
SiO2 wt% Used in Experiment0 wt%0.5 wt%2.5 wt%5 wt%15 wt%
BCDA-3,4'-ODA/SiO2 MMM64 ± 2°64 ± 2°65 ± 3°70 ± 3°72 ± 2°
BCDA-1,3,3-APB/SiO2 MMM75 ± 1°72 ± 1°71 ± 2°70 ± 1°66 ± 2°
# 3.2. Swelling Effects The degree of swelling may be used as an indication of the affinity between polymer and a certain solvent [62]. The experimental results of pure alicyclic PI membranes on the degree of swelling for the three solvents are in the following order: for BCDA-3,4'-ODA, n-butanol $(61\%) >$ ethanol $(57\%) >$ water $(47\%)$ ; for BCDA-1,3,3-APB, n-butanol $(55\%) >$ ethanol $(25\%) >$ water $(10\%)$ . As indicated in the literature [63] using aromatic PI membranes, the degree of swelling for $90~\mathrm{wt}\%$ aqueous ethanol solution was higher than that for $10~\mathrm{wt}\%$ aqueous ethanol solution; that is, the aromatic PIs exhibited a stronger affinity with ethanol molecules than water molecules. In [62], the pure membranes of commercial PIs such as Matrimid, Torlon, and P84 showed the solvent uptake ratio in an order of n-butanol $>$ isopropanol $>$ ethanol $>$ water. Our results of alicyclic PI membranes had the same tendency on solvent affinity as theirs. To theoretically investigate the membrane solubility, Hansen solubility parameters [64] are usually adopted. The total solubility parameter $\delta$ $(\mathrm{(MJ / m^3)^{1 / 2})}$ includes three terms: dispersion $(\delta_{\mathrm{d}})$ , polarity $(\delta_{\mathrm{p}})$ , and hydrogen bonding $(\delta_{\mathrm{h}})$ : $$ \delta^ {2} = \delta_ {\mathrm {d}} ^ {2} + \delta_ {\mathrm {p}} ^ {2} + \delta_ {\mathrm {h}} ^ {2}. \tag {7} $$ The values of Hansen solubility parameters for common polymers could be found from the literature, but not for the polymers with more complex, uncommon, or newly synthesized monomers. In our case, the Van-Krevelen-Hoftyzer (VKH) method [65] was used to decompose the functional groups of alicyclic PI and then sum up the group contributions for evaluating the solubility parameter value, as follows: $$ \delta_ {\mathrm {d}} = \sum \frac {\mathrm {F} _ {\mathrm {d i}}}{\mathrm {V}}, \tag {8} $$ $$ \delta_ {\mathrm {P}} = \frac {\sqrt {\sum \mathrm {F} _ {\mathrm {p i}} ^ {2}}}{\mathrm {V}}, \tag {9} $$ $$ \delta_ {\mathrm {h}} = \sqrt {\frac {\sum \mathrm {E} _ {\mathrm {h i}}}{\mathrm {V}}}, \tag {10} $$ $$ V = \frac {M}{\rho}, \tag {11} $$ where $\mathrm{F_{di}}$ : dispersion term $(\mathrm{J}^{1 / 2}\mathrm{cm}^{3 / 2}\mathrm{mol}^{-1})$ ; $\mathrm{F_{pi}}$ : polar term $(\mathrm{J}^{1 / 2}\mathrm{cm}^{3 / 2}\mathrm{mol}^{-1})$ ; $\mathrm{E_{hi}}$ : hydrogen bonding term (J/mol); V: molar volume $(\mathrm{cm}^3 /\mathrm{mol})$ ; $\rho$ : density $(\mathrm{g / cm^3})$ ; M: molecular weight of the repeating unit (g/mol). In order to judge the affinity between solvent and polymer, $\delta_{\mathrm{m,c}}$ , the interaction force between polymer (m) and solvent (c) was calculated from the following equation [66-68]: $$ \delta_ {\mathrm {m}, \mathrm {c}} = \sqrt {\left(\delta_ {\mathrm {d} , \mathrm {m}} - \delta_ {\mathrm {d} , \mathrm {c}}\right) ^ {2} + \left(\delta_ {\mathrm {p} , \mathrm {m}} - \delta_ {\mathrm {p} , \mathrm {c}}\right) ^ {2} + \left(\delta_ {\mathrm {h} , \mathrm {m}} - \delta_ {\mathrm {h} , \mathrm {c}}\right) ^ {2}}. \tag {12} $$ The values of $\delta_{\mathrm{d}}$ , $\delta_{\mathrm{p}}$ , $\delta_{\mathrm{h}}$ , $\delta$ , and $\delta_{\mathrm{m,c}}$ for alicyclic PIs, $\mathrm{SiO}_2$ , and three solvents are presented in Table 3. In general, the smaller the $\delta_{\mathrm{m,c}}$ value, the higher the affinity between polymer and solvent [66]. Table 3. Values of Hansen solubility parameters for alicyclic PIs, $\mathrm{SiO}_2$ , and three solvents.
δDδPδHδ
BCDA-3,4'-ODA15.36.38.318.5
BCDA-1,3,3-APB16.15.38.018.7
SiO21827.52943.8
Butanol165.715.823.2
Ethanol15.88.819.426.5
Water15.51642.347.8
δm,cBCDA-3,4'-ODABCDA-1,3,3-APBSiO2
Butanol7.67.825.6
Ethanol11.411.921.1
Water35.435.917.8
As shown in Table 3, BCDA-3,4'-ODA had slightly lower $\delta_{\mathrm{m,c}}$ values for the three solvents than BCDA-1,3,3-APB. That is, BCDA-3,4'-ODA should have had slightly better affinities with the three solvents than BCDA-1,3,3-APB, which matched the results of solvent swelling effect. Moreover, the order of $\delta_{\mathrm{m,c}}$ value for both PIs was butanol $<$ ethanol $<$ water. In reverse order, the affinity between alicyclic PI and solvent became butanol $>$ ethanol $>$ water. This sequence was consistent with the order of the solvent swelling degree obtained in this work. On the other hand, the Hansen solubility parameters of $\mathrm{SiO}_2$ and its related $\delta_{\mathrm{m,c}}$ values for the three solvents were also evaluated, and the data are reported in Table 3. The order of $\delta_{\mathrm{m,c}}$ value for $\mathrm{SiO}_2$ was butanol $>$ ethanol $>$ water, in an entirely opposite order to alicyclic PIs. These data suggest that $\mathrm{SiO}_2$ should be more hydrophilic than alicyclic PIs. However, the results of water contact angle in Table 2 indicate that the incorporation of $\mathrm{SiO}_2$ nanoparticles in MMM exhibited the opposite tendency on hydrophilicity for a different PI matrix. Thus, the solvent solubilities of $\mathrm{PI} / \mathrm{SiO}_2$ MMMs were investigated via measuring their solvent swelling degrees. The results are listed in Table 4. In the case of BCDA-3, $4^{\prime}$ -ODA/ $\mathrm{SiO}_2$ MMMs, the increase in $\mathrm{SiO}_2$ wt% led to the decrease in the degree of swelling of pure water but an increase in pure butanol solubility. In contrast, an opposite trend was attained for BCDA-1,3,3-APB/ $\mathrm{SiO}_2$ MMMs. These trends are in good agreement with the water contact angle results. Conclusively, the BCDA-3, $4^{\prime}$ -ODA/ $\mathrm{SiO}_2$ MMMs became less hydrophilic due to the incorporation of $\mathrm{SiO}_2$ nanoparticles, whereas BCDA-1,3,3-APB/ $\mathrm{SiO}_2$ MMMs were more hydrophilic with $\mathrm{SiO}_2$ loading. The phenomena on solvent swelling degrees displayed in the literature adopting chitosan/ $\mathrm{SiO}_2$ xerogel MMMs [59] were similar to our BCDA-1,3,3-APB/ $\mathrm{SiO}_2$ MMM case, since the tendency of water contact angle was the same. Table 4. Degrees of swelling for pristine alicylic PI membranes and their related $\mathrm{{PI}}/{\mathrm{{SiO}}}_{2}$ mixed matrix membranes.
SiO2 wt% Used in Experiment0 wt%0.5 wt%2.5 wt%5 wt%15 wt%
BCDA-3,4'-ODA/SiO2 MMMwater47%48%47%44%38%
n-butanol61%69%79%83%92%
BCDA-1,3,3-APB/SiO2 MMMwater10%19%22%24%39%
n-butanol55%48%37%34%31%
# 3.3. Pervaporation Performance The pervaporation process of $85\mathrm{wt}\%$ n-butanol aqueous solution was conducted at $40^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ using one piece of membrane disc (effective surface area: $19.63~\mathrm{cm}^2$ ) in this study. The results of the total permeation flux and separation factor are illustrated in Figure 6. Due to the membrane thickness variation, the normalized permeation flux $(J_{\mathrm{N}})$ in terms of $30~\mu \mathrm{m}$ (the average membrane thickness) was expressed. For pristine alicyclic PI membranes, BCDA-3,4'-ODA exhibited both a larger flux and a higher separation factor than BCDA-1,3,3-APB. ![待补充图片](https://placeholder.pics/svg/1x1/FFFFFF/FFFFFF?text=暂无图片) ![待补充图片](https://placeholder.pics/svg/1x1/FFFFFF/FFFFFF?text=暂无图片) ![待补充图片](https://placeholder.pics/svg/1x1/FFFFFF/FFFFFF?text=暂无图片) Figure 6. Pervaporation performance of pristine alicyclic PI membranes and their related $\mathrm{PI} / \mathrm{SiO}_2$ mixed matrix membranes. ![待补充图片](https://placeholder.pics/svg/1x1/FFFFFF/FFFFFF?text=暂无图片) Worth reminding is that solution-diffusion is the principal separation mechanism for pervaporation through a dense membrane [14,15]. In the previous section discussing the swelling effect, BCDA-3,4'-ODA certainly revealed higher solvent affinities than BCDA-1,3,3-APB. To further understand the diffusion discrepancy for both alicyclic PIs, fractional free volume (FFV) was evaluated. FFV is usually applied to quantify the gap between polymer segments. As FFV increases, solvent molecules pass through the membrane more easily, resulting in an increased flux. The calculation of FFV is as follows [69]: $$ \mathrm {F F V} = \frac {\mathrm {V} - \mathrm {V} _ {0}}{\mathrm {V}} = 1 - \rho_ {\mathrm {m}} \mathrm {V} _ {0}, \tag {13} $$ $$ \mathrm {V} _ {0} = 1. 3 \mathrm {V} _ {\mathrm {W}}, \tag {14} $$ where V: specific volume of polymer $(= 1 / \rho_{\mathrm{m}})$ $(\mathrm{cm}^3 /\mathrm{mol})$ . $\mathrm{V_0}$ : occupied volume of polymer at $0\mathrm{K}$ $(\mathrm{cm}^3 /\mathrm{mol})$ . $\rho_{\mathrm{m}}$ : molar density of polymer $(\mathrm{mol} / \mathrm{cm}^{3})$ . $\mathrm{V_W}$ : Van der Waals volume $(\mathrm{cm}^3 /\mathrm{mol})$ . $\mathrm{V_W}$ value was estimated by Bondi's model of group contribution [70]. The results verified that BCDA-3,4'-ODA had a larger FFV value (0.189) than BCDA-1,3,3-APB (0.171). More free volume allowed more solvent molecules to pass through the PI membrane with BCDA-3,4'-ODA. Since both the solvent solubility and diffusivity of BCDA-3,4'-ODA were higher than BCDA-1,3,3-APB, the resulting total permeation flux of BCDA-3,4'-ODA was two-fold that of BCDA-1,3,3-APB. In addition, the individual fluxes of water and n-butanol were separately plotted in Figure 6. For both pristine PI membranes, the water flux was much larger than the butanol flux, such that the total flux was close to the individual water flux. This phenomenon is totally opposite to their swelling results in Table 4, where n-butanol could be uptaken and sorbed into the pure PI membrane more than water. The contradictory trend between solvent swelling degree and permeation flux for water/alcohol systems was also reported for the chitosan membrane [59] and BPDA-PI membrane [63]. The molecular dynamic diameter of water (2.7 Å) is smaller than butanol (5.5 Å) [59] so that water has a faster diffusion capability than butanol. Diffusion accelerated more water molecules across the dense PI membrane, resulting in a high water percentage at the permeate flux. Consequently, a large separation factor of water over butanol was yielded. The separation ability of our PI membranes was mainly accredited to the diffusion mechanism. The dominant diffusivity process of pervaporation was also found in the literature [58] using the PVA/nano silica composite membranes. To evaluate the effect of $\mathrm{SiO_2}$ incorporation, the pervaporation results of the alicyclic $\mathrm{PI} / \mathrm{SiO}_2$ MMMs are also presented in Figure 6. The tendency of total flux was close to that of individual water flux because the water content in permeate was very big. Moreover, both kinds of $\mathrm{PI} / \mathrm{SiO}_2$ MMMs revealed similar effects on permeation fluxes, although they had opposite effects on membrane hydrophilicity (analyzed from the data of water contact angle and degree of swelling). The water flux values in most MMM cases were less than that of pristine PI membrane. On the other hand, with the increased $\mathrm{SiO_2}$ content, the n-butanol flux decreased first and then increased for both types of $\mathrm{PI} / \mathrm{SiO}_2$ MMMs. The variation in solvent flux may be contributed from three effects: The first one is the degree of swelling [59], and a reminder that both types of $\mathrm{PI} / \mathrm{SiO}_2$ MMMs exhibited the opposite tendency in solvent swelling degree. The second effect is the restriction on liquid transport due to the blockage of nonporous $\mathrm{SiO_2}$ nanoparticles [71], which would reduce the solvent flux. The third possible effect is the creation of non-selective transport paths owing to the void formation from $\mathrm{SiO_2}$ nanoparticle aggregation [43,56,58,59], which usually occurs at higher loading content. The combination of these phenomena led to different trends for water flux and n-butanol flux. Water flux may be interfered more with the addition of nonporous $\mathrm{SiO_2}$ nanoparticles. Since diffusion transport took over the permeation rate, this influence had even overwhelmed the effect of increasing water affinity for $\mathrm{SiO_2}$ loading in BCDA-1,3,3-APB/ $\mathrm{SiO_2}$ MMMs. As for n-butanol flux, the combined effect resulted in the minimal flux appearing at $0.5\mathrm{wt}\%$ $\mathrm{SiO_2}$ loading for BCDA-3,4'-ODA/ $\mathrm{SiO_2}$ MMMs and $2.5\mathrm{wt}\%$ for BCDA-1,3,3-APB/ $\mathrm{SiO_2}$ MMMs. In the aspect of the separation factor, both $\mathrm{PI} / \mathrm{SiO}_2$ MMMs displayed a similar effect: the separation factor increased first and then decreased with the increasing filler loading. Similar trends were also indicated in the literatures [56,59]. In Figure 6, the optimal separation factor occurred at $0.5\mathrm{wt}\%$ for BCDA-3,4'-ODA/SiO $_2$ MMMs and $2.5\mathrm{wt}\%$ for BCDA-1,3,3-APB/SiO $_2$ MMMs, which were identical to the $\mathrm{SiO}_2$ content for the minimal n-butanol flux in each case. The separation factor was raised mainly by the decrease in n-butanol flux. In addition, the incorporation of $\mathrm{SiO}_2$ nanoparticles was more effective for the case of BCDA-1,3,3-APB/SiO $_2$ MMMs, especially to enhance the separation factor at 3-5.7-fold. By combining the two important pervaporation outcomes (total flux and separation factor) together, the PSI value for each membrane was calculated as the index of separation efficiency. The PSI data are plotted in Figure 7. Based on the PSI performance, the optimal $\mathrm{SiO}_2$ content was $0.5\mathrm{wt}\%$ for BCDA-3,4'-ODA/SiO $_2$ MMMs and $5\mathrm{wt}\%$ for BCDA-1,3,3-APB/SiO $_2$ MMMs. In the case of BCDA-1,3,3-APB, the maximum PSI value did not occur at the maximal separation factor ( $2.5\mathrm{wt}\%$ from Figure 6). The value of total permeation flux had a more significant contribution on the evaluation of PSI in this case, due to the fact that the separation factor values for BCDA-1,3,3-APB/ $\mathrm{SiO}_2$ MMMs were not high. However, at the optimal filler content, the PSI value of BCDA-1,3,3-APB/5 wt% $\mathrm{SiO}_2$ MMM was raised 3.6-fold of pure PI membrane, while that of BCDA-3,4'-ODA/0.5 wt% $\mathrm{SiO}_2$ MMM was enhanced by only $8\%$ . ![待补充图片](https://placeholder.pics/svg/1x1/FFFFFF/FFFFFF?text=暂无图片) Figure 7. Variation in PSI (pervaporation separation index) with the increasing $\mathrm{SiO_2}$ wt% in $\mathrm{PI} / \mathrm{SiO}_2$ mixed matrix membrane. ![待补充图片](https://placeholder.pics/svg/1x1/FFFFFF/FFFFFF?text=暂无图片) The water/n-butanol pervaporation performances using the as-prepared alicyclic $\mathrm{PI} / \mathrm{SiO}_2$ MMMs in this study are compared to those of other membranes reported in the literatures, as listed in Table 5. Note that the overall separation efficiency (PSI) of BCDA-3,4'-ODA-based membranes is about 10-30 times higher than that of BCDA-1,3,3-APB-based membranes. Only the PSI values of pristine alicyclic PI membrane and the $\mathrm{PI} / \mathrm{SiO}_2$ MMM with optimal $\mathrm{SiO}_2$ content are presented in Table 5 for comparison. The PSI values of BCDA-1,3,3-APB-based membranes are only comparable to those of silica membranes, but they have better permeation flux than silica, pristine PBI, Torlon-based, P84-based, and PPSU-based membranes. On the other hand, the BCDA-3,4'-ODA-based membranes are superior to commercial PI/30% hPIM-1, PPSU-based, PVA cross-linked by citric acid, and silica membranes in both permeation flux and PSI. Consequently, large permeation flux is the chief feature of our alicyclic $\mathrm{PI} / \mathrm{SiO}_2$ MMMs. Table 5. Comparison of water/n-butanol pervaporation performance of alicyclic PI/SiO $_2$ mixed matrix membranes with literatures.
